coredata.dc:description: Photocapacitors are a breakthrough in energy storage and generation, storing and generating solar energy in several ways. Sustainable technology and satisfying the energy needs of a rapidly changing planet depend on their progress. A hydrothermal technique was used to synthesize carbon quantum dots-ornamental MoS₂ nanopetals on reduced graphene oxide (rGO). XRD, Raman, and XPS validated the hybrid nanomaterial's phase purity, composition, and oxidation state. Morphological research showed MoS₂ nanopetals producing a flower-like structure with evenly dispersed carbon quantum dots on a thin, twisted sheet. As a counter electrode in dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs), this hybrid material increased efficiency by 6.7 % and photovoltaic characteristics. An asymmetric supercapacitor made from the hybrid material and activated carbon had 166.6 F/g specific capacitance at 5 mV/s and 81.5 % capacitance retention after 5000 charge-discharge cycles. The combined photocapacitor device, combining DSSC and supercapacitor, obtained 4.4 % overall efficiency and 34 % storage efficiency. The MoS₂ nanopetals-based electrode shows promise for photocapacitor applications, potentially improving future flexible and wearable technologies.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photocapacitors are a breakthrough in energy storage and generation, storing and generating solar energy in several ways. Sustainable technology and satisfying the energy needs of a rapidly changing planet depend on their progress. A hydrothermal technique was used to synthesize carbon quantum dots-ornamental MoS₂ nanopetals on reduced graphene oxide (rGO). XRD, Raman, and XPS validated the hybrid nanomaterial's phase purity, composition, and oxidation state. Morphological research showed MoS₂ nanopetals producing a flower-like structure with evenly dispersed carbon quantum dots on a thin, twisted sheet. As a counter electrode in dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs), this hybrid material increased efficiency by 6.7 % and photovoltaic characteristics. An asymmetric supercapacitor made from the hybrid material and activated carbon had 166.6 F/g specific capacitance at 5 mV/s and 81.5 % capacitance retention after 5000 charge-discharge cycles. The combined photocapacitor device, combining DSSC and supercapacitor, obtained 4.4 % overall efficiency and 34 % storage efficiency. The MoS₂ nanopetals-based electrode shows promise for photocapacitor applications, potentially improving future flexible and wearable technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of integrated photo capacitor device (IPSC)

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In the recent technological era energy is the most precious and essential requirement in day-to-day life. The world is still looking for the best, most viable, more economical and simple techniques for the generation and storage of energy. The combination of energy generation and energy storage under a single platform is the most wanted technology to satisfy the modern and smart technological world [1,2]. This hybrid technology is most important and it attracted many researchers which is also known as self-charging devices [3,4]. In addition, self-charging devices are comprised of energy harvesting and storage system. Third generation solar cells, piezoelectric and triboelectric sources are incorporated as energy harvesters and Li-S, Sodium ion batteries [5] and supercapacitors are used as energy accumulation devices [6,7]. However, compared to the other self-charging devices photo capacitors has gained high attraction because of this low-cost and compactable energy production for the futuristic devices [8]. With the high utilization of solar energy, the dye-sensitized solar cell is adapted owing to high stability towards the environmental atmosphere. Supercapacitors are implemented in the energy storage part due to their high cyclic stability and simple fabrication process [9–11].

In recent decades, dye sensitized solar cells (DSSC) have been noted as one of the feasible and superlative energy generating devices, which convert solar light into electrical current. DSSC has considered to be a good alternative source compared to other generation solar cells because of the easy fabrication process, cheaper price, environmental sustainability, indoor / low light operations and it has long power conversion stability compared to perovskite solar cells [12–14]. DSSC mainly contains a photoanode, sensitizer (dye), electrolyte and counter electrode. The dye molecules were adsorbed by photoanode, when the light incident on the surface, the photo-generated charges are created and then produced charges were separated spontaneously and it is transferred to the counter electrode [15–17]. However, DSSC faces some hindrances in counter electrodes, Pt is a well-known counter electrode but owing to its higher cost and slow degradation towards corrosive electrolytes. In the energy storage devices, supercapacitors (SP) are recently developing research areas because of their rapid charge-discharge abilities and it is widely used in different fields like electronic gadgets, electrical vehicles, toys and data storage systems etc. [18]. SP can be classified into three types depending on their storage mechanisms those are electric double layer capacitors (EDLC), pseudo capacitors and hybrid capacitors. The pseudo capacitor consists of transition metal oxides and conducting polymers for the electrode materials and the hybrid capacitors contain both carbon based materials with the EDLC nature and metal oxide with pseudo capacitive properties [19].

Transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) nanostructures have recently been proposed as a new class of potential candidates for energy harvesting and storage application owing to their layered structure and high catalytic activity [20,21]. In particular, MoS2 is one of the promising materials in TMDs family and received a lot of interest among researchers due to its interesting and tunable properties of low cost, electrochemical stability and high abundance [22]. Moreover, the extremely thin layers provide a tremendous amount of surface area and considerable active edge sites in various types of MoS2 nanostructures, which may lead to increased electrochemical performance. The numerous MoS2 morphology has been studied recently such as MoS2 nanosheets [23], MoS2 nanoribbons [24] and nanoflowers [25,26] which provides interesting electrochemical properties. Hence, the optimization of surface morphology and structure of MoS2 nanosheets offers effective results in DSSC and supercapacitor applications. The carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are semiconductor nanocrystals that are 0-dimensional nanoparticles, recognized in recent years, due to their interesting properties of good solubility, chemical stability, biocompatibility and economical cost of synthesis [27]. The quantum dots are used in a variety of applications such as bioimaging, drug delivery, sensor, solar cells and supercapacitors because of their fascinating properties [28]. The primary core of CQDs consists of sp2 and sp3 carbon atoms, and its surface holds the active functional group sites. It could be used as an electron transport medium or bridge to improve the conductivity of CQDs composites [29]. Graphene and its derivatives have shown to be extremely efficient in a wide range of applications. According to research, when metal oxides and sulfides (MO/MS) are applied to the surface of these quantum dots it gives the improved characteristics to its applications [30]. Hong Ha Thi Vu et al. prepared a composition of TiO2 nanofiber with spectral convertors in order to improvise the overall efficiency of the DSSC device [31]. Ramkumar Sekar et al. fabricated a dye sensitized solar cells which had an enhanced efficiency when ZnS dopant was composited with TiO2 [32]. Nan Zhao et al. fabricated an aqueous symmetric supercapacitor where MoO3 electrode was used as both cathode and anode and its electrochemical performances were carried out [33]. Nan Zhao et al. fabricated a high-performance supercapacitor device employing NiCo2O4 composite electrode owing to an outstanding electrochemical property [34]. Lixin Song et al. reviewed about the electrochemical performances of photo-assisted rechargeable batteries and both of its experimental as well as theoretical characterization techniques were studied [35]. Kang Yan et al. fabricated stable Zn batteries using salt-based polymer solid electrolytes for rapid phase transition [36]. Nan Zhao et al. discussed about the recent advancements in metal oxides based fibrous supercapacitors [37].

In this current work, the hybrid composite was created using MoS2, carbon quantum dots and rGO owing to their superficial properties. The MoS2 nanopetals are synthesized using a single-step hydrothermal process, and carbon quantum dots and reduced graphene oxide is prepared through the microwave synthesis method and modified hummers method, respectively. The as prepared carbon quantum dots are decorated on 2-D layered MoS2 nanopetals and rGO matrix. The as prepared sample was undergone for the investigation of physical, chemical and morphological properties and samples were employed to the counter electrode for the DSSC and the electrochemical performances were studied. Well-performed sample is employed as the working electrode to fabricate the asymmetric supercapacitor device. In addition, to study the characteristics of self-charging device, the fabricated asymmetric supercapacitor device and DSSC is integrated physically and the photo capacitor performance was studied.

Typically, 12 mg of molybdenum trioxide was mixed in 25 mL of double-distilled water and agitated for 30 minutes to create MoS2 nanopetals. After adding 0.12 g of urea to the solution and constantly stirring it for 10 minutes at room temperature, a Sulphur source of thioacetamide, weighing around 14 mg, was added. The mixture was further swirled for fifteen minutes. The prepared solution, designated as solution A, is moved to an autoclave with a Teflon lining and heated to 200 °C for 12 h in a hot air oven. Following the heat reaction, the precipitate is collected and repeatedly rinsed with ethanol in an autoclave setup to cool room temperature. Additionally, the final product was dried for 12 hours at 80 °C. The produced sample was then ground and kept for further examination in a vacuum desiccator and it is coded as BM.

The rGO method is typically prepared as follows: A solution of H2SO4/H3PO4 (90:10) mL was prepared and it was placed at a temperature of 5 °C for 7 h in an ice bath and 500 mg of graphite powder were gradually added to the H2SO4/H3PO4 solution before being allowed to sit in an ice bath for one and a half hours. The preceding acid combination was then progressively mixed with KMnO4, and the ice bath was kept at 20 °C for one hour. This caused the solution to become greenish black, and it was then heated to 70 °C for twenty-four hours while being vigorously stirred. After 24 h, the greenish-black solution develops a light brownish tint and is cooled to room temperature. 400 mL of distilled water was mixed with the produced solution, and 5 mL of H2O2 (35 %) was added dropwise to finish the reaction. As graphite oxide forms, the colour of the solution becomes golden yellow. The solution was then treated with distilled water and rinsed until the pH reached a neutral level. For the formation of rGO, 500 mL of distilled water was combined with the resulting graphite oxide, and the sample was probe sonicated for five hours [38].

In order to produce carbon quantum dots, 8 millimolar of citric acid is added to 20 mL of double-distilled water. Then, 8.09 millimolar of ethylenediamine, which is a reducing agent, is added to the solution, and it is agitated under room temperature conditions for twenty minutes. A microwave oven operating at 900 watts for five minutes is used to heat the solution that has been prepared. Following the solution's transformation into a brownish colour, it was collected and put through dialysis for a period of twenty-four hours in order to get pure carbon quantum dots [39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs ornamented MoS2 nanopetals on rGO
ce:section.ce:para: For the purpose of fabricating the CQDs decorated MoS2 nanopetals on rGO, the 5-weight percent of reduced graphene oxide that had been created and the 1 millilitre of carbon quantum dots that had been synthesized simultaneously were combined in solution A and agitated for thirty minutes at room temperature. Following the transfer of the prepared solution into the Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, which was heated to 200 degrees Celsius for a period of twelve hours, the final product was collected and cleaned in order to the final composite, which was given the name MRQ. The same technique is performed in order to make a MoS2@rGO composite. This involves adding rGO by itself to solution A, and the sample that is produced is referred to as (MR). In order to create MoS2 that is decorated with carbon quantum dots, the same technique is used, which involves combining 1 millilitre of CQDs with solution A. The code for the sample in its prepared state is (MQ). In Fig. 1, we see an illustration of the preparation and synthesis process.

The Dye Sensitized Solar cells was fabricated using FTO glasses as substrate where, 2×2 cm FTO glasses where ultra sonically cleaned with soap solution followed by DD water and finally with ethanol. The Cleaned FTO glasses were kept inside UV illuminator for eliminating Organic contaminants. The Photo anode was prepared by making a slurry out of 600 mg P25 TiO2, 100 mg polyethylene Oxide, 150 mg Polyethylene glycol, 150 µL Acetyl Acetone, 25 µL Triton-X 100, 2 mL Distilled water, made into a fine slurry by grinding them for 115 minutes using Mortar and pestle. The Slurry was then coated over masked FTO glass through Dr. Blade method followed by Sintering them for 30 minutes at 500 °C. The TiO2 coated FTO was then soaked in N719 dye for 12 h in a dark atmosphere. The soaked FTO plates where then rinsed with ethanol to remove excess dye solution. 0.3 mM, of N719 dye powder was mixed in absolute ethanol and sonicated for 30 minutes in an amber bottle. The electrolyte solution Iodide/Triiodide was prepared with 32 mg of iodide balls, 183 mL of 4-tert butylpyridine, 5 mg Polyethylene oxide, 35 mg of polyethylene glycol, added to an amber glass vile and stirred in dark room atmosphere. After 12 hours of stirring, 33 mg of lithium iodide is added to the electrolyte mixture and again stirred for 12 h.

The Counter electrode is prepared by grinding 95 mg of Active material, 5 mg of polyvinylidene fluoride with NMP (N-Methyl-2-Pyrrolidone) together for 15 minutes. The resulting paste is then coated over a cleaned FTO glass through Dr. Blade method and allowed to dry in an oven for 12 h at 80 °C. The DSSC was fabricated by sandwiching the two electrodes together (Counter electrode and Photo anode) with gel electrolyte (Iodide/Triiodide) dropped in-between them. The Images of Fabricated DSSC counter Electrodes has been provided in the Fig.S1. (Supporting information)

The X-ray diffraction pattern of as prepared MoS2 nanopetals (BM), carbon quantum dots ornamented MoS2 (MQ), MoS2@rGO (MR) and carbon quantum dots ornamented MoS2 nanopetals on rGO (MRQ) was shown in Fig. 2. The major XRD peaks present in 32.9°, 35.8°, 39.5°, 43.4°, 49.3°, and 58.8° corresponds to the (100), (102), (103), (006), (105) and (008) crystal planes of MoS2 and it is coherent with the ICDD card no 075-1539 [40]. The presented MoS2 was in a hexagonal crystal system, P63/mmc space group and major peaks existed in all the samples showing that MoS2 nanopetals were predominantly presented. The peak broadening in the sample refers to the random and disordered orientation of MoS2 sheets [41]. The presence of a primary broad peak at 26° in MR, MQ and MRQ suggests the presence of rGO and carbon quantum dots in MoS2 [42].

Raman spectroscopy is the most important tool to observe the interaction kinetics of the two-dimensional layered structure Fig. 3 represents the Raman spectra of as prepared samples BM, MQ, MR and MRQ. The presence of MoS2 is confirmed by two peaks located at 378.3 cm−1 it is corresponding to the E1 2 g in-plane vibration of Mo and S atoms and 402.7 cm−1 is respective to A1g out-plane vibration of S atoms. The intensity of A1g peak is stronger than E1 2 g this shows that its rich in edge-terminated structure [43] and these 2 peaks confirm the occurrence of 2H phase of MoS2. The additional two peaks were observed in MQ, MR and MRQ samples, 1352 cm−1 and 1589 cm−1 concerning the sp3 hybridized D-band and sp2 hybridized G -band, respectively. These bands expose the existence of graphitic nanocarbon and the ID/IG ratio is about 1.1 it refers that the defect sites in carbon which show the presence of rGO and carbon quantum dots [44]. There are no additional peaks observed in BM which confirms that only MoS2 nanopetals were presented in the sample.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was used in order to explore the oxidation states, valance states, and surface chemical composition of the elements present in the composite material. The survey spectrum of the carbon quantum dots adorned MoS2 nanopetals on rGO (MRQ) is shown in Fig. 4(a), which provides a representation of the spectrum. The survey spectrum reveals the presence of peaks associated with S 2p, Mo 3d, C 1s, Mo 3p, and O 1s. Furthermore, there were no other peaks seen, which provides an explanation for the sample's high level of purity. In Fig. 4(b), the core level spectrum displays the Mo 3d area, which is responsible for the peaks at 229 eV and 232.5 eV, respectively. These peaks are ascribed to the Mo4+ 3d5/2 and Mo4+ 3d3/2 resonances, respectively [25]. Both the weak peak of S 2s, which was seen at 225 eV, and the extra peak, which was found to be 235.7 eV and is attributed to the Mo6+ 3d5/2, are indicative of the 2H phase of MoS2 [45]. The deconvolution peaks of S 2s that are shown in Fig. 4(c) correlate to the spectral peaks that are located at 161.1 eV, 162.6 eV, and 164.2 eV respectively. These spectral peaks correspond to the S2p1/2, S2p3/2, and S-C, which demonstrates that S2- is the predominant oxidation state. The peaks for CO, C-O, and C-OH are exposed in the O 1 s spectra that are displayed in Fig. 4(d). These peaks are located at 530.43, 531.5, and 532.79 eV, respectively. The high-resolution spectrum of carbon 1 s is shown in Fig. 4(e), which demonstrates the OC-O peak at 288.52 eV for the oxidized carbon species and the C-C peak at 284.4 eV, which is a very intense peak that indicates the existence of a high carbon content in rGO.

The morphological analysis was performed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) for the synthesized sample MoS2 nanopetals (BM), carbon quantum dots ornamented MoS2 (MQ), MoS2@rGO (MR) and carbon quantum dots ornamented MoS2 nanopetals on rGO (MRQ), the corresponding SEM images were displayed in Fig. 5(a–d). The MoS2 nanopetals, which were created evenly, were linked to one another in order to create a flower-like structure that was fluffy and had a thin layer thickness of MoS2 as shown in Fig. 5(a). The thickness of the nanopetals is around 10 nanometers, and the size of the flower-like structure is approximately 500 nanometers. Nanopetals did not undergo any changes in their morphology as a result of the incorporation of carbon quantum dots and rGO; in fact, their morphology remained unchanged. Because of their smaller size, carbon quantum dots are not visible in the SEM picture (Fig. 5(b)). The addition of rGO can be seen in Fig. 5(c & d) MR and MRQ, respectively. The presence of carbon quantum dots is not noticed in the SEM image. The energy dispersive spectroscopy of MoS2 nanopetals that have been decorated with carbon quantum dots and placed on rGO (MRQ) is shown in Fig. 6(a). Presented in the hybrid system in its as-prepared state, the EDAX spectrum indicated the existence of Mo, S, C, and O elements. The weight and atomic percentage of the elements reveal the composition of the components. Due to the fact that the elemental mapping of MRQ gives strong evidence of elements Mo, S, C, and O that were presented equally, it is obvious that the addition of carbon quantum dots and rGO was thoroughly mixed and presented in a homogeneous manner in the sample MRQ.

Images obtained using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) of the sample MRQ, which are displayed in Fig. 7(a–i), provide additional confirmation of the structural morphology. As can be seen in Fig. 7(a), the presence of nanopetals resulted in the formation of a structure that resembled a crumpled flower. The chaotic structure of the nanopetals, which suggests the presence of unsaturated active sites and the development of defects, was seen throughout the experiment. The presence of rGO sheets can be seen in Fig. 7(b, c). The figure illustrates that MoS2 nanopetals are placed above rGO sheets, and the thickness and transparency of the material confirm that rGO was formed. As can be seen in Fig. 7(d), the carbon quantum dots are attached to the surface of the MoS2 nanopetals. The quantum dots have a size of approximately 7–10 nm and are equally attached to the surface of the MoS2. It is confirmed that the carbon quantum dots are present by the interlayer spacing of 0.21 nm. It was shown in Fig. 7(f) that the interlayer spacing of MoS2 nanopetals rose to 0.63 nm. This is beneficial for improved performance in electrochemical investigations because to the quick ion intercalation that occurs. SAED is responsible for the delivery of the planes (100), (002), (102), and (006), which are in perfect alignment with the XRD pattern of MoS2. Additionally, the diffuse rings demonstrate the existence of carbon-based components such as rGO and carbon quantum dots.

The dye-sensitized solar cells were created utilizing four different materials as the counter electrode: MoS2 nanopetals (BM), carbon quantum dots decorated MoS2 (MQ), MoS2 combined with reduced graphene oxide (MR), and carbon quantum dots decorated MoS2 nanopetals on reduced graphene oxide (MRQ). The Fig. 8(a) displays the photocurrent density and photovoltage (J–V) curve of the manufactured DSSC devices. The corresponding photovoltaic parameters are included in a Table1. The BM, as it was made, exhibits a photoconversion efficiency of 3.2 %. This is achieved with a short circuit current density (JSC) of 9.8 mA/cm2, a fill factor (FF) of 0.46 %, and an open circuit voltage (Voc) of 0.71 V. The photoconversion efficiency of 3.5 % is shown by the MQ, with a current density (JSC) of 10.23 mA/cm2, a fill factor (FF) of 0.48 %, and an open-circuit voltage (VOC) of 0.72 V. The synthesized MR achieved an efficiency of 4.9 %, with a current density of 17 mA/cm2 (JSC), a fill factor of 0.48 % (FF), and a voltage of 0.73 V (VOC). On the other hand, the MRQ that was made showed an improved efficiency of 6.7 %. Photovoltaics parameters for the repeated devices of MRQ, MR, MQ, BM along with its standard deviation has been provided in the Table. S1. present in the supporting information. The sample MRQ has been compared with the reference platinum counter electrode and the J-V and EIS curve has been provided in the Fig. S3. (Supporting Information). Fig.S2. in the supporting information provides the J-V curves for fabricated DSSC for repeated devices BM, MQ, MR, MRQ
The parameters likewise saw an improvement, with JSC rising by 17.2 mA/cm2, FF increasing by 0.54 %, and VOC remaining at 0.73 V.

Because of the combined effect and high edge sites of MoS2 nanopetals with rGO and carbon quantum dots, MRQ is able to deliver better photovoltaic performance. This comes about as a result of the combination of these substances. On rGO, the carbon quantum dots attached the MoS2 nanopetals, which resembled little flowers. This hybrid nanoarchitecture gives the better charge carriers in charge transfer network, and morphological investigation has proven that this structure is correct. In order to facilitate the diffusion of electrolyte ions, the counter electrode and electrolyte contact offers a significant amount of surface area [46].

In addition, electrochemical impedance spectroscopic (EIS) study is carried out from 0.1 Hz to 10 kHz in order to investigate the electrochemical processes that are carried out by produced DSSCs. Examples of this analysis may be seen in Fig. 8(b). A series resistance (Rs) of 16.82 Ω is provided by the BM, along with a charge transfer resistance (RCT1) of 50.62 Ω and a differential resistance (RCT1) of 46.15 Ω. The sample MQ yields the values of 11.8, 43.13, and 48.67 Ω for Rs, RCT1, and RCT2, respectively. The second sample MR demonstrates that the Rs is 16.5 Ω, the RCT1 is 38.89 Ω, and the RCT2 is 41.62 Ω. A reduced resistance Rs of 10.7 Ω, an RCT1 of 25.49 Ω, and an RCT2 of 44.64 Ω are all offered by the MRQ in its current state of preparation. Because the CQDs that are chemisorbed in the MoS2 nanopetals and in the presence of the rGO layer play a critical part in the transportation of electrolytes to increase the catalytic activity, the charge transfer resistance is reduced. This is done in order to achieve the lower charge transfer resistance. Fig. 8(c) depicts the photocurrent response graph, which provides the repeatability of the short circuit density during on-off cycles under light irradiation on DSSC for a total of four cycles with a ten-second gap between each cycle. The proportion of photocurrent that is attenuated is 5 %, and it is stabilized after each cycle by using the equivalent photocurrent that corresponds to the JSC. The photostability graph is shown in Fig. 8(d), and similar investigations are also carried out for a duration of 200 seconds while being illuminated by continuous light. The photostability reported after 200 Sec are 87, 96, 96 and 98 % for MoS2 nanopetals (BM), carbon quantum dots ornamented MoS2 (MQ), MoS2@rGO (MR), carbon quantum dots ornamented MoS2 nanopetals on rGO (MRQ), respectively.

KCl saturated Ag/AgCl was used as the reference electrode, platinum wire was used as the counter electrode, and 3 M KOH was used as the electrolytic medium. This three-electrode setup was established in order to explore the electrochemical redox behavior of the BM, MQ, MR, and MRQ. The results of this investigation are shown in Fig. (a–d). Cyclic Voltametric (CV) analysis of all of the electrodes that were manufactured reveals the typical battery-like behavior, which is characterized by the appearance of strong oxidation and reduction peaks at various potentials. These peaks are caused by the insertion and desertion of electrolytic ions into the porous and layered structure of the electrodes. When the sweep rates are increased from 10 mV/s to 100 mV/s, the redox peaks of all of the electrodes stay the same. This demonstrates that the electrodes have high reversibility and rate capacity. As a result of the presence of a wide variety of functional groups that are adsorbed on the surface, the CV examination of the MR electrode reveals a greater integral area than the MB and MQ electrodes. This is because these functional groups offer enhanced electrochemical active sites with their presence.

There are multiple redox active sites owing to the large active surface area, synergistic effect, and better electrical conductivity, which results in delivering enhanced electrochemical activity. In addition, the area occupied by the MRQ is considerably larger than the area occupied by the BM, MQ, and MR. This is because the number of redox active sites is quite higher. According to the CV profiles of BM, the specific capacity (Cs) was determined to be 230.41, 206.75, 157.716, 130.82, 116.148, and 106.88 F/g. On the other hand, the Cs values that were obtained for MQ were 232.48, 149.27, 140.6, 130.9, 120.67, and 111.3 F/g, which corresponded to 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, and 100 mV/s, respectively. To identify the exact charge accumulation mechanism on the electrode structure, Trasatti method was used to differentiate the EDLC contribution and the capacitive contribution as,

The resultant capacitive contribution qi (%) of the electrodes 80.7 %, 90.8, 92.4, and 93.1 % and the diffusion contribution qo (%) percentage was found to be 19.3 %, 9.2 %, 7.6 %, and 6.9 % respectively for the BM, MQ, MR, (shown in Fig. S4) and MRQ respectively and the comparison bar chart shown in
Fig. 9(e–g). Fig.S4. in the supporting information provides the Trasatti plot of BM, MQ, and MR. Every sample has a GCD profile that displays typical non-linear charge-discharge patterns, which is evidence that the samples are similar to batteries. Furthermore, the GCD data that were obtained are in excellent agreement with the CV curves [48]. The MRQ electrode, in comparison to the BM, MQ, and MR electrodes, has a discharge duration that is much longer. This suggests that the MRQ electrode possesses greater electrochemical activity due to the synergy effect, which results in the formation of multivalent cations (Fig. 10).

It is possible to evaluate the internal resistance and capacitive performance of the electrodes based on BM, MQ, MR, and MRQ by using EIS analysis, as shown in
Fig. 11(a). According to the Nyquist plot, the solution resistance (Rs) values of BM, MQ, MR, and MRQ were determined to be 0.17 Ω, 0.15 Ω, 0.06 Ω, and 0.04 Ω, respectively. The charge transfer resistance (RCT) values for these solutions were 10.2 Ω, 7.63 Ω, 12.5 Ω, and 3.72 Ω, respectively. Furthermore, the MRQ was shown to have a decreased internal impedance and high charge transport kinetics, which demonstrates its higher electrical conductivity and quick ionic diffusion, both of which contribute to the enhanced electrochemical activity. In addition, the cyclic stability test was performed at a current density of 10 A/g in order to guarantee the electrode stability of MRQ. The results of this test demonstrated that the capacitive retention was 88.9 % even after 3000 charge discharge cycles, as seen in Fig. 11(b) (Fig. 12).

In order to study the electrochemical characteristics of the asymmetric two electrode supercapacitor device, CV, EIS, and GCD investigations were performed in a KOH gel electrolyte. The device was built for practical application utilizing MRQ as the positive electrode and AC as the negative electrode, respectively. A quasi-rectangular peak with a hump was detected as a result of the combined effect of EDLC and pseudocapacitive behaviour, as shown by the CV curves. From the CV curve, it was determined that the specific capacitance was 166.6 F/g when the scan rate was 5 mV/s and the potential window was 1.5 V. This was found to be within the broad potential window. Even at higher scan rates, the form of the CV curve does not change, which demonstrates the greater reversibility of the SP device that was manufactured as well as the influence of particular capacitance at various scan rates. The device's charge-discharge study reveals a symmetric triangle-shaped profile with a low infrared drop. This is a reflection of the quick charge transfer kinetics, low internal resistance, which results in a longer charge-discharge time, and high specific capacitance. In particular, the device that was manufactured is capable of delivering a longer discharge duration even when subjected to very high current densities, which demonstrates the device's stability. In addition to this, the SP device displays a low voltage drop even when the current density is growing, which is a sign of its high electrode stability and double layer activity. In addition, the Nyquist plot demonstrated the low solution resistance (Rs) and charge transfer resistance (RCT) values of 0.38 Ω and 11.2 Ω, respectively. These values indicate the extraordinary charge transfer kinetics and the modest equivalent series resistance (ESR) that are associated with the supercapacitor device. It is necessary to show the electrode stability and reversibility test in order to assess the practical use of the supercapacitor device. This may be accomplished by repeatedly performing the charge-discharge profile at a constant current density for a number of thousands of cycles. In this study, the constructed SP device was subjected to a capacitive retention test for a total of three thousand consecutive charge-discharge cycles. The resulting capacitive retention was determined to be 81.5 % maintained from their original capacitance at a constant current density of ten amperes per gram. It is the energy density and power density that are the most important criteria to consider when evaluating the performance of the SP in relation to its commercial constraints in comparison to other SP devices. Specifically, the specific capacitance and cell potential are the primary factors that determine the energy density of the SP device that was manufactured. Within the scope of this research, the SP device in its current state exhibits a wider cell potential of around 1.5 V, and the energy density that corresponds to it was determined to be 29.25 Wh/kg. It is noteworthy that the SP gadget that was manufactured had a power density of around 2700 W/kg.

The dye-sensitized solar cells (TiO2/N719/3I-I3-/MRQ) and two-electrode Asymmetric supercapacitor (MRQ/KOH/Activated carbon) were linked together ( Fig. 13(a) From the photoanode of DSSC, the negative connection was linked to the positive side of the asymmetric supercapacitor (HSC), and from the counter electrode, the positive connection was connected to the negative side. Finally, the wired integrated dye sensitized solar cell and asymmetric solar cell (DSSC@HSC) was connected into two electrode modes in potentiostat to study it. In Fig. 13(b), the photovoltage response against time with varied current densities while lighting the light, voltage, and current from the DSSC and charging the asymmetric supercapacitors was exhibited. After photo charging illumination was turned off and DSSC was kept in the dark, photo charged supercapacitor device underwent galvanostatic discharge with various current densities. After increasing the current density to 0.2, 0.5, and 0.7 mA cm−2, the discharge time decreased to 446.6, 145.7, and 52.1 s, respectively. The photovoltage was lowered to zero in 694.5 s for 0.1 mA cm Self-discharge of the combined DSSC@HSC without external current is seen in Fig. 13(b). The gadget was charged by light for 100 seconds, then switched off and discharged. This steady voltage discharge lasts 1500 sec at 0.25 V. Fig. 13(c) shows the galvanostatic discharge of the integrated DSSC@HSC and DSSC alone at 0.1 mA cm−2. The integrated DSSC@HSC has a longer discharge time, while the DSSC alone discharges the photovoltage suddenly, showing that the DSSC alone cannot hold the photovoltage. The overall photo-electrical conversion and storage efficiency (ηoverall) of the integrated DSSC@HSC is calculated by the following formula.

The storage parameters, energy density and power density of the integrated DSSC@HSC were given in Table2. The present integrated DSSC@HSC device fabricated using carbon quantum dots ornamented MoS2 nanopetals on rGO the 4.4 % and 65.18 % of ηoverall and ηstorage, respectively. Z.Yang et.al., tested the integrated device using a multiwalled carbon nanotube film this device provided 0.79 % of overall efficiency (ηoverall) and 34 % of storage efficiency (ηstorage) compared to our integrated device delivered improved overall and storage efficiency [50].

Further for individual device comparison such has solar cell - counter electrode and supercapacitor behaviours of electrode materials the power conversion efficiency (PCE) and specific capacitance, capacitive retention was tabulated in
Tables 3 and 4 respectively. The comparison tables clearly shows that the as prepared CQDs decorated MoS2 nanopetals on rGO sample shows good impact in individual device performance.

Hydrothermal synthesis of carbon quantum dots-ornamented MoS2 nanopetals on rGO was achieved. The hybrid nanomaterial's XRD, Raman, and XPS physio-chemical characteristics indicate its phase purity, composition, and oxidation state. The XRD data indicates the presence of a primary broad peak at 26° in MR, MQ and MRQ which suggests the presence of rGO and carbon quantum dots in MoS2. From the Raman spectra, it is analysed that there is a presence of additional two peaks in MQ, MR and MRQ samples corresponding to D-band and G -band. Morphological examination shows MoS2 nanopetals in a thin tangled sheet that aggregates into a flower-like shape. Carbon quantum dots were equally anchored in MoS2 nanopetals on reduced graphene oxide. The developed counter electrode boosted photovoltaic parameters and efficiency by 6.7 % when evaluated with DSSC. The prepared sample and activated carbon are used to make an asymmetric supercapacitor with 166.6 F/g specific capacitance at 5 mV/s and slow discharge. The device has 29.25 Wh/kg energy density, 2700 W/kg power density, and 81.5 % capacitance retention after 5000 charge-discharge cycles. The supercapacitor and DSSC devices were combined to create an integrated photo capacitor device, resulting in 4.4 % overall efficiency and 34 % storage efficiency. The MoS2 nanopetals electrode shows promise for photo capacitor applications, enhancing the performance of flexible and wearable electronics.
